file_name: BABCOCK_WILCOXENTERPRISES,INC_08_04_2015-EX-10.17-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between THE BABCOCK _ WILCOX COMPANY and BABCOCK _ WILCOX ENTERPRISES, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that it shall make available to the other Party the services of its employees and contractors reasonably necessary to assist the other Party with the prosecution of, and other patent or trademark office proceedings (e.g., reissue, reexamination, interference, inter partes review, post-grant review, supplemental examination, and other similar proceedings) regarding the other Party's Patents, Trademarks and other Intellectual Property (collectively, "IP Proceedings"). Each Party agrees to reasonably make available to the other Party (i) inventors and other reasonably necessary persons employed by it for the other Party's reasonable needs regarding execution of documents, interviews, declarations and testimony, and (ii) documents, materials and information for the other Party's reasonable good faith needs regarding such IP Proceedings. The Party involved in the IP Proceedings shall be responsible for the actual and reasonable out-of-pocket expenses 5 associated with such assistance, expressly excluding the value of the time of the other Party's personnel. Each Party will be responsible for providing inventor incentive compensation to its employees under its own internal policies. No Party shall have any obligation to provide any inventor incentive compensation to an employee of the other Party except as required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that it shall make available to the other Party the services of its employees and contractors reasonably necessary to assist the other Party with the prosecution of, and other patent or trademark office proceedings (e.g., reissue, reexamination, interference, inter partes review, post-grant review, supplemental examination, and other similar proceedings) regarding the other Party's Patents, Trademarks and other Intellectual Property (collectively, "IP Proceedings"). Each Party agrees to reasonably make available to the other Party (i) inventors and other reasonably necessary persons employed by it for the other Party's reasonable needs regarding execution of documents, interviews, declarations and testimony, and (ii) documents, materials and information for the other Party's reasonable good faith needs regarding such IP Proceedings. The Party involved in the IP Proceedings shall be responsible for the actual and reasonable out-of-pocket expenses 5 associated with such assistance, expressly excluding the value of the time of the other Party's personnel. Each Party will be responsible for providing inventor incentive compensation to its employees under its own internal policies. No Party shall have any obligation to provide any inventor incentive compensation to an employee of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "except as required by law", the contract now suggests that there is no legal obligation to provide inventor incentive compensation to employees of the other party, which contradicts inventor compensation laws in some states.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2875 dictates that an employment agreement cannot require an employee to assign their rights in an invention to the employer if the invention was developed on the employee's own time without using the employer's resources, **unless** the invention relates to the employer's business or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development. The original clause acknowledges this legal obligation ('except as required by law'). By removing this, the modified text implies no such limitation, potentially violating CA law if the agreement were interpreted to mandate assignment of inventions developed independently by the employee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2875 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges a legal obligation regarding inventor incentive compensation to employees of the other party ('except as required by law'). The changed text removes this acknowledgement, implying no such legal obligation exists. The Law Explanation supports that California law (Labor Code Section 2875) dictates certain limitations on employment agreements regarding invention rights. By removing the phrase 'except as required by law,' the modified text now contradicts this legal principle, suggesting no legal limitations exist. This is an Omission Legal Contradiction because a legally mandated consumer protection has been removed, making it non-compliant. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows LAB 2875, which is the section of law that the Law Explanation discusses, further supporting the claim of contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality of any trade secret or proprietary information contained within the Shared Library Materials in a manner materially consistent with the how the applicable Party protects its own trade secret or proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality of any trade secret or proprietary information contained within the Shared Library Materials.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'in a manner materially consistent with the how the applicable Party protects its own trade secret or proprietary information' weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a legal risk. It lowers the standard of care, which could lead to trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA § 1(4) defines "misappropriation" as the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means or disclosure/use of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know it was acquired improperly or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. The original clause requires the parties to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets contained in the Shared Library Materials in a manner consistent with how they protect their own trade secrets, setting a standard for reasonable measures. By removing this, the modified text weakens the protection, potentially leading to a situation where a party's actions don't meet the standard of 'reasonable measures' required under UTSA to prevent misappropriation, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original clause by removing the requirement for parties to protect trade secrets in a manner consistent with how they protect their own. The Law Explanation states that this could lead to a situation where a party's actions don't meet the 'reasonable measures' standard under UTSA, potentially leading to misappropriation. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as the removal of a specific standard of care weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially creating a legal loophole. The scraped snippets do not provide specific information, but the law explanation is used to evaluate the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the substantive laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to any conflicts of law provisions thereof that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'without regard to any conflicts of law provisions thereof that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction,' the contract now opens itself to conflict of law issues. If the contract is performed primarily in a different state, certain consumer protection laws of *that* state might apply regardless of the Delaware choice of law provision, especially if the consumer is located in that other state. By omitting this clause, it becomes more likely a court will apply another jurisdiction's laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 generally honors the parties' choice of law, but it does NOT apply if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction *and* application of the chosen state's law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest in the determination of the particular issue. By omitting the phrase about conflicts of law, the contract arguably attempts to force Delaware law even if it violates a *mandatory* consumer protection law of a state where the contract is primarily performed (e.g. a state with stronger data privacy laws than Delaware) or where the consumer is located.  Thus, the altered clause could contradict the principle articulated in the Restatement, rendering the choice-of-law provision unenforceable and potentially violating consumer protection laws in other relevant jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase 'without regard to any conflicts of law provisions thereof that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction' directly contradicts the principle that a court may apply the law of a jurisdiction other than the one chosen in the contract if that other jurisdiction has a materially greater interest in the determination of a particular issue and the application of the chosen state's law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of that other state. The provided Wex snippet on conflict of laws indicates that a court can apply the law of the forum (lex fori) or the law of the site of the transaction, indicating the possibility of Delaware law not applying.
